steps.title: Visit the nearest Police Station
steps.description: Go to the police station within whose jurisdiction the offence occurred. Under Section 154 CrPC, every police station is bound to register an FIR for cognizable offences.

steps.title: Provide written or oral information
steps.description: Narrate the incident to the Station House Officer (SHO) or the officer in-charge. The information can be given orally (which will be transcribed) or in writing. Mention all relevant details: date, time, place, accused (if known), witnesses, and facts.

steps.title: Get FIR copy (Section 154(2))
steps.description: Under Section 154(2) CrPC, a copy of the FIR shall be given to the informant free of charge. Insist on receiving this copy.

steps.title: If FIR is refused
steps.description: If police refuse to register FIR, send written complaint to the Superintendent of Police (Section 154(3)). If still not registered, approach the Magistrate under Section 156(3) CrPC to direct the police to register FIR and investigate.

steps.title: Investigation begins
steps.description: Once FIR is registered, the investigating officer (IO) will begin investigation under Section 156-173 CrPC. This includes visiting the crime scene, recording statements, collecting evidence, and arresting the accused if needed.

steps.title: Determine bail eligibility
steps.description: Check whether the offence is bailable or non-bailable. For bailable offences (Section 436 CrPC), bail is a right. For non-bailable offences (Section 437), bail is at the court's discretion.

steps.title: File bail application
steps.description: If bailable — apply at the police station itself. If non-bailable — file bail application before the Magistrate or Sessions Court. Include grounds: no flight risk, no tampering with evidence, personal bonds, health issues, etc.

steps.title: Court hearing
steps.description: The court hears arguments from both prosecution and defence. The court considers: nature and gravity of the offence, criminal antecedents, likelihood of fleeing justice, likelihood of tampering with evidence.

steps.title: Bail granted/rejected
steps.description: If granted, the accused furnishes a bail bond and surety as directed. If rejected, appeal to the next higher court (Sessions Court or High Court under Section 439 CrPC).

steps.title: Anticipatory Bail (Section 438)
steps.description: If arrest is apprehended but not yet made, apply for anticipatory bail under Section 438 CrPC before the Sessions Court or High Court. The court may grant bail with conditions.

steps.title: Determine cause of action and jurisdiction
steps.description: Identify the civil wrong (breach of contract, property dispute, recovery of money, injunction, etc.) and determine the appropriate court (District Court, Munsif Court) based on pecuniary and territorial jurisdiction under CPC Section 15-20.

steps.title: Draft and file the plaint
steps.description: Draft the plaint as per Order VII CPC containing: facts of the case, cause of action, relief sought, court fees stamp. File in the appropriate civil court.

steps.title: Summons issued to defendant
steps.description: After filing, the court issues summons to the defendant under Order V CPC, giving them 30 days to file written statement (Order VIII).

steps.title: Written statement and issues framing
steps.description: Defendant files written statement. Court then frames issues (disputed points of fact and law) under Order XIV CPC.

steps.title: Evidence and trial
steps.description: Both parties lead evidence (documentary and oral) under Order XVIII CPC. Examination-in-chief, cross-examination, and re-examination of witnesses.

steps.title: Arguments and judgment
steps.description: After evidence, both parties present final arguments. Court delivers judgment under Section 33 CPC. Aggrieved party can appeal within 30/90 days.

steps.title: Identify the writ and grounds
steps.description: Determine which writ applies — Habeas Corpus (unlawful detention), Mandamus (compel public duty), Certiorari (quash order), Prohibition (prevent excess jurisdiction), Quo Warranto (challenge authority). Article 226 (High Court) or Article 32 (Supreme Court).

steps.title: Draft the writ petition
steps.description: Draft setting out: facts, fundamental rights violated (Part III of Constitution), cause of action, prayers/reliefs sought, supporting documents and affidavit.

steps.title: File before the High Court / Supreme Court
steps.description: File the writ petition along with court fees, certified copies of impugned orders, and affidavit. Apply for interim relief (stay order) if urgency exists.

steps.title: Court hearing and orders
steps.description: Court issues notice to respondent, hears both parties, and passes appropriate orders — interim or final.

steps.title: Send legal notice to the company
steps.description: Before filing complaint, send a legal notice under Consumer Protection Act 2019 to the company/service provider detailing the deficiency in service or defective goods, and the relief sought.

steps.title: File complaint with appropriate forum
steps.description: If value ≤ ₹1 crore: District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission. If ₹1-10 crore: State Commission. If > ₹10 crore: National Commission. File under Section 35 of Consumer Protection Act 2019.
steps.timeline: Within 2 years of cause of action

steps.title: Attach supporting documents
steps.description: Include: purchase receipts/invoices, communication records, photographs of defective goods, medical reports if applicable, copy of legal notice sent, and any other relevant evidence.

steps.title: Hearing and mediation
steps.description: The Commission may refer the matter to mediation first. If mediation fails, the matter proceeds to hearing where both parties present evidence and arguments.

steps.title: Order and execution
steps.description: The Commission passes orders for compensation, replacement, refund, or other relief. Orders are executable as civil court decrees. Appeal lies to the next higher commission.

steps.title: Determine grounds for divorce
steps.description: Under Section 13 of Hindu Marriage Act 1955: cruelty, desertion (2 years), conversion, unsoundness of mind, venereal disease, renunciation, presumption of death (7 years). Mutual consent under Section 13B requires 1 year separation.

steps.title: File petition in Family Court
steps.description: File divorce petition in the Family Court having jurisdiction where marriage was solemnized, or where respondent resides, or where couple last resided together.

steps.title: Court-directed mediation/counseling
steps.description: The court typically refers the matter for mediation/counseling as per Section 9 of Family Courts Act. At least 6-month cooling period for mutual consent divorce.

steps.title: Trial or second motion
steps.description: If contested: evidence stage with examination of witnesses. If mutual consent: second motion filed after 6 months (and before 18 months) to confirm both parties still consent.

steps.title: Decree of divorce
steps.description: Court passes decree of divorce. Matters of maintenance (Section 25 HMA), child custody (Section 26 HMA), and property division are decided. Appeal to High Court within 30 days.

steps.title: Identify the public authority
steps.description: Determine which government body holds the information you need. Every public authority must designate a Public Information Officer (PIO) under Section 5 of RTI Act 2005.

steps.title: Draft and submit RTI application
steps.description: Write application to the PIO mentioning: your name, contact details, specific information sought. Pay ₹10 fee (by postal order, demand draft, or cash). No need to give reason for seeking information.

steps.title: Receive response
steps.description: PIO must respond within 30 days (48 hours if life/liberty is involved) under Section 7. If information is from another public authority, PIO transfers within 5 days.

steps.title: First Appeal
steps.description: If dissatisfied or no response received, file first appeal to the First Appellate Authority (senior officer of the same department) within 30 days under Section 19(1).

steps.title: Second Appeal to Information Commission
steps.description: If first appeal is unsatisfactory, file second appeal to Central/State Information Commission within 90 days under Section 19(3). Commission can impose penalty of ₹250/day on defaulting PIO.
steps.timeline: Within 90 days of first appeal decision
